1: "Why, LORD, do you stand far off? Why do you hide yourself in times of trouble?" — the cry of divine absence that reaches its culmination in Christ's cross. Psalms 22:1 is the canonical expression: "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" — which Jesus cried in Matthew 27:46 and Mark 15:34. Psalms 10:1 belongs to the same family of desolation prayers. Christ inhabited the depth of this question — not merely citing it but living it, experiencing the actual absence of divine consolation so that the abandonment of sin could be fully borne and exhausted.
2: The wicked pursuing the poor in arrogance describes the spiritual dynamic that opposed Christ. His entire ministry was characterized by the powerful targeting the powerless — and targeting him for his alignment with them. The religious establishment pursued Jesus for associating with tax collectors and sinners (Luke 5:30), and ultimately pursued him to the cross (John 11:47-53). In being hunted by the arrogant, Christ identified himself with every oppressed person throughout history.
3: "The wicked man boasts about the cravings of his heart; he blesses the greedy and reviles the LORD" — the idolatry of self-exaltation. Paul's analysis in Romans 1:21-23 identifies this as the root human sin: "they neither glorified him as God nor gave thanks to him... their thinking became futile." Christ exposed this pattern most directly in his confrontations with the Pharisees (Matt 23) and in the parables of self-exaltation (Luke 18:9-14 — the Pharisee who prayed about his own virtues).
4: "In his pride the wicked man does not seek him; in all his thoughts there is no room for God" — the practical atheism of self-sufficiency. Jesus diagnosed this in the parable of the rich fool (Luke 12:16-21): the man whose plans had no room for God. The incarnation is God's response to a world with no room for him — he entered it anyway, born into a borrowed stable (Luke 2:7). The rejection of Christ is the definitive form of this verse: in the climactic moment of divine presence, there was "no room."
5: "His ways are always prosperous; your laws are rejected by him; he sneers at all his enemies" — the apparent prosperity of the wicked that deceives. Jesus addressed this in the parable of the rich man and Lazarus (Luke 16:19-31): visible prosperity and suffering are not reliable indicators of divine favor. The cross is the ultimate inversion: the one who appeared cursed (Galatians 3:13 — "cursed is everyone who is hung on a pole") was in fact carrying the blessing of the world.
6: "He says to himself, 'Nothing will ever shake me.' He swears, 'No one will ever do me harm'" — the false security of self-confidence. Christ warned against this repeatedly: "But God said to him, 'You fool! This very night your life will be demanded from you'" (Luke 12:20). The one who built on rock (Christ's words) would stand; the one who built on sand (self-sufficiency) would fall (Matthew 7:24-27). The whole security of the believer is in Christ, not self.
7: "His mouth is full of lies and threats; trouble and evil are under his tongue" — James 3:6-8 develops the theological significance of the corrupt tongue: "the tongue is a fire, a world of evil... it corrupts the whole body." Christ diagnosed the tongue's evil as a symptom of a corrupt heart (Matthew 15:18: "the things that come out of a person's mouth come from the heart"). Paul quotes a cluster of passages including Psalms 10:7 in Romans 3:14 as part of his diagnosis of universal human corruption requiring Christ's righteousness.
8: The wicked lying in wait to murder the innocent — this pattern culminated in the cross. Psalms 10:8-10 pictures an ambush: hidden waiting, then sudden attack on the unsuspecting. The betrayal of Jesus by Judas (Matthew 26:14-16, 47-50) and the night-time arrest in Gethsemane follow exactly this pattern — the chief priests and teachers waited for "an opportunity" (Luke 22:6), then struck in the night. Christ walked into the ambush knowingly, laying down his life rather than having it taken (John 10:18).
9: "He lies in wait like a lion in cover; he lies in wait to catch the helpless" — the lion-imagery for the enemy connects to 1 Peter 5:8 ("your enemy the devil prowls around like a roaring lion looking for someone to devour") and Revelation 5:5 (Christ as "the Lion of the tribe of Judah" who has triumphed). The evil lion who hunts the helpless is overcome by the Lion who gave himself to be hunted, so that through his willing surrender he defeated the predator (Hebrews 2:14-15: "by his death he might break the power of him who holds the power of death").
10: "His victims are crushed, they collapse; they fall under his strength" — the condition of those crushed by sin and its consequences, which Christ specifically came to address. Luke 4:18 (Isaiah 61:1): "He has sent me to proclaim freedom for the prisoners and recovery of sight for the blind, to set the oppressed free." Christ's entire ministry was oriented toward those who had been crushed by the enemy — the demon-possessed, the disease-ridden, the social outcasts, the spiritually broken.
11: "He says to himself, 'God will never notice; he covers his face and never sees'" — the denial of divine omniscience that sin requires. Christ exposed this illusion consistently: "Nothing is hidden that will not be disclosed" (Luke 8:17); "your Father, who sees what is done in secret, will reward you" (Matthew 6:4, 6, 18). The cross made visible what was hidden — it publicly displayed "the rulers and authorities" (Colossians 2:15) and their character, "triumphing over them by the cross." What sin tries to conceal, the cross unmasks.
12: "Arise, LORD! Lift up your hand, O God. Do not forget the helpless" — the resurrection prayer at the center of the psalm. As in Psalms 9:19-20, the petition for divine arising reaches its answer in the resurrection of Christ. God "lifted up his hand" against sin and death in raising Christ from the dead (Ephesians 1:19-20: "the incomparably great power... which he exerted when he raised Christ from the dead"). The helpless — those who cannot save themselves — are the primary beneficiaries of this arising.
13: "Why does the wicked man revile God? Why does he say to himself, 'He won't call me to account'?" — the fundamental failure of accountability before God that the cross addresses. Christ's death was the definitive calling-to-account: all sin was held accountable at Calvary (2 Corinthians 5:21 — "God made him who had no sin to be sin for us"). The resurrection is the announcement that the account has been settled and the one who bore accountability for sin has been vindicated.
14: "But you, God, see the trouble of the afflicted; you consider their grief and take it in hand" — YHWH as the one who takes in hand the cause of the afflicted. The incarnation is the supreme taking-in-hand: "In all their distress he too was distressed... he lifted them up and carried them all the days of old" (Isaiah 63:9). Christ taking human suffering "in hand" — literally, physically, in his own body (Isaiah 53:4: "Surely he took up our pain and bore our suffering"; 1 Peter 2:24) — is the fulfillment of this verse.
15: "Break the arm of the wicked man; call the evildoer to account for his wickedness that would not otherwise be found out" — the prayer for the defeat of the enemy's power. Christ "broke the arm" of the wicked through the cross (1 John 3:8: "The reason the Son of God appeared was to destroy the devil's work"; Colossians 2:15: "having disarmed the powers and authorities, he made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them by the cross"). The enemy's power is broken; its complete accounting awaits the final judgment (Revelation 20:10).
16: "The LORD is King forever and ever; the nations will perish from his land" — Christ's eternal kingship declared. Revelation 11:15: "The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Messiah, and he will reign for ever and ever." The nations that opposed Christ's kingdom will be purged from the renewed creation — not through ethnic exclusion but through the exclusion of rebellion itself. The new earth will have no room for the opposition described in Psalms 10:1-15.
17: "You, LORD, hear the desire of the afflicted; you encourage them, and you listen to their cry" — Christ's ministry precisely exemplified this divine listening. He stopped for blind Bartimaeus when the crowd tried to silence him (Mark 10:46-52). He stopped for the hemorrhaging woman who touched his garment in a crowd (Mark 5:25-34). He heard the one leper who returned to give thanks (Luke 17:11-19). The Son's attentiveness to the afflicted was the visible, embodied form of this verse.
18: "...defending the fatherless and the oppressed, so that mere earthly mortals will never again strike terror" — the eschatological promise: the protection of the vulnerable, and the end of terror from human oppressors. This finds its fulfillment in the new creation (Revelation 21:4: "there will be no more death or mourning or crying or pain") and its first-fruits in Christ's kingdom where the excluded are welcomed (Matthew 19:14 — "let the children come to me"; Galatians 3:28 — "there is neither slave nor free, neither male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus").
